claims.claim: During safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells, CH4 was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC, and CH4 concentration was positively correlated with SoC.
claims.quote: During safety venting, CH4 was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC. At 25% SoC, the % volume increase observed for CH4 was 5.50%, compared to 0.15% CO2, 0.02% CO, 0.35% C2H6 and 0% H2. At 100% SoC, the % volume for CH4 was 6.8% compared to 0.29% CO2, 0.01% CO, 0.45% C2H6 and 0% H2.

claims.claim: No H2 was detected during safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells across all tested SoC.
claims.quote: in our tests, no H2 was detected during safety venting. A similar observation was reported by J. Siegel, who noted that H2 is not detected consistently in first venting events.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, H2 peak % volume increases ranged from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC, showing a strong positive correlation between H2 concentration and SoC.
claims.quote: Our data revealed a strong correlation between H2 concentration and SoC, with peak % volume increases ranging from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: Increasing SoC from 25% to 100% produced a 3 to 5-fold increase in total gas volume in both air and N2 atmospheres; in air, gas volume increased from 0.8 L to 3.9 L, and in N2 from 2.2 L to 6.5 L.
claims.quote: For both the air and N2 based tests, we observed a 3-5-fold jump in the total volume of gas produced when the SoC was increased from 25% to 100%. In air, the total gas generated increased from 0.8 L at 25% SoC to 3.9 L at 100% SoC; for the tests conducted in N2, 2.2 L of gas was produced at 25% SoC, which increased to 6.5 L at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: The onset gas detection window (duration between start of safety venting and start of thermal runaway) was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (142 s) compared to 5% SoC (262 s), and this window decreases with increasing SoC because threshold temperatures for safety venting and thermal runaway decrease with increasing SoC.
claims.quote: the window of opportunity to detect vent gas prior to thermal runaway was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (t = 142 s) when compared to 5% SoC (t = 262 s) under the same test conditions. These windows are fundamentally influenced by the threshold temperature values for safety venting and thermal runaway, which decrease with increasing SoC. At 100% SoC, thermal runaway can initiate at a temperature up to 60 ◦C lower than at 5% SoC.

claims.claim: Fluorinated compounds in the amounts common in battery vent gases do not have any effects on ignition delay time or macroscopic combustion characteristics.
claims.quote: Comparing the ignition delay time for similar mixtures with (Case 2) and without (Case 3) fluorinated compounds, it is clear that the presence of F does not affect the ignition.

claims.claim: Ignition of Li-ion battery vent gas mixtures is not sensitive to oxygen content and occurs at approximately the same ignition delay time in oxygen-depleted conditions as at high oxygen availability.
claims.quote: Note that ignition does not require a high oxygen content but occurs at about the same delay time in the oxygen-depleted case.

claims.claim: At temperatures below about 550°C, ignition of battery vent gas mixtures requires a long time or an external ignition source.
claims.quote: Ignition is highly temperature dependent and at temperatures below about 550 C ignition of the gas mixtures will require a long time or an external ignition source.

claims.claim: At 730°C, carbonate-containing vent gas mixtures ignite up to an order of magnitude slower than the most reactive H2/CO-rich mixtures.
claims.quote: it is clearly seen that the carbonate-containing mixtures (Case 1–3) ignite as much as an order of magnitude slower than the most reactive mixtures, among which Case 6 where the reactants consist of almost fifty per cent hydrogen gas ignite the fastest

claims.claim: The laminar burning velocity of Case 5 (SOC 100%) is about 50% higher than that of Case 4 (SOC 30%) for gases from the same NMC/LMO battery, demonstrating that combustion properties are highly dependent on SOC.
claims.quote: the laminar burning velocity for Case 5 (SOC 100%) is about 50% higher than that for Case 4 (SOC 30%), which clearly shows that the combustion properties of gases from a given battery are highly dependent on the SOC during the thermal event leading up to the gas production.

claims.claim: A partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (bag volumes of 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (bag volume of 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject a two-car garage door.
claims.quote: a partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject the two-car garage door.

claims.claim: The synTREG volumes sufficient to eject the garage door correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, whereas 25% of the well-mixed LFL is typically used when assessing explosion hazard precipitation.
claims.quote: These synTREG volumes correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, where 25% of the well-mixed LFL is used when assessing the precipitation of an explosion hazard.

claims.claim: Significant structural damage including cracked wooden wall studs, dislodged drywall, deformed man-door latch plates, and ejected garage doors was observed at peak internal pressures of approximately 2.51–2.79 kPa, which are approximately 50% below the Jarrett and Baker thresholds for minor structural damage.
claims.quote: The 826 L NCA and 320 L LFP gas bag experiments achieved peak internal pressures of 2.79 kPa and 2.51 kPa, respectively. In both cases, the maximum pressure was approximately 50% less than the threshold established by Jarrett or Baker.

claims.claim: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas, which is attributed to the higher burning velocity of the LFP gas.
claims.quote: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas. This is expected due to the higher burning velocity of the gas.

claims.claim: In each bag experiment case considered, the Mulpuru vented explosion model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise compared to experimental results, indicating it may provide conservative estimates for explosion hazard design calculations.
claims.quote: In each bag case considered, the model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise when compared to experimental results. This demonstrates that the Mulpuru model may provide more conservative estimates of the rate of pressure rise for explosion hazard design calculations.

claims.claim: For the 3302 L NCA gas bag experiment, the maximum overpressure was 4.12 kPa, which was lower than the 6.25 kPa measured in the 1651 L gas bag experiment, with the decrease attributed to early pressure relief through the garage door acting as a deflagration vent.
claims.quote: the largest tested bag volume of 3302 L deviates from the model trend, having a maximum overpressure of 4.12 kPa compared to 6.25 kPa for the 1651 L gas bag. This behavior may be attributed to the low-strength garage door, which relieved pressure and vented the still-combusting gas cloud, similar to a deflagration panel.
